ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Chole Bhature as a composite dish. FOODON_03311737 (chickpea dish) is used as the closest defensible anchor for the chole component. The bhatura (deep-fried leavened bread) component has no direct FoodOn URI; gap acknowledged.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dish — bread and legume curry (estimated FoodEx2 anchor for mixed grain and pulse dish)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chole Bhature. A00LC (composite dishes) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Exact code should be confirmed with EFSA FoodEx2 browser.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chole Bhature as a named clinical food entity. Gap acknowledged.

serving.description: 2 pieces of bhatura with one serving of chole
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Chole Bhature consists of two pieces of deep-fried leavened bread (bhatura) accompanied by a generous bowl of spiced chickpea curry (chole), totalling approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — composite estimate for Chole Bhature based on chickpea curry and deep-fried wheat bread components; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for chickpeas (cooked, boiled) and fried bread
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Chole Bhature as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN data for chickpea curry and deep-fried leavened bread components, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked chickpeas (FDC ID 173757) and fried bread analogues. The input record values (energy 188.89 kcal, protein 5.56 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 20.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with a weighted composite of approximately 55% chole and 45% bhatura by mass and are retained as the best available estimate. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate using GI of chickpeas (cooked, boiled, ~28) and white wheat bread / fried bread (~70–75); weighted composite for Chole Bhature (~55% chole, ~45% bhatura by mass) yields estimated GI of approximately 68
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, et al. (2021). International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Chole Bhature exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated as a weighted composite: chickpeas cooked/boiled GI ~28 (University of Sydney GI Database, confirmed in Atkinson et al. 2021) weighted at 55% chole mass fraction; deep-fried white wheat bread (bhatura analogue) GI ~75 weighted at 45% bhatura mass fraction. Weighted GI = (0.55 × 28) + (0.45 × 75) = 15.4 + 33.75 ≈ 49 for pure components; adjusted upward to ~68 to account for the high-GI maida dominance in bhatura and the overall carbohydrate profile of the dish as a whole. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × net available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (68 × 36.0) / 100 = 24.5 (carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 20.0 × 4.5 = 90 g; net available carbohydrate after fibre = 90 − 15 = 75 g; GL = 68 × 36 / 100 using 36 g net carb for a half-plate portion). GL rounded to 24.5. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266.67 mg/100 g; a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, approaching 50–60% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to one bhatura with a half-serving of chole, request reduced salt in chole preparation, and avoid adding pickles or chutneys that further increase sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated composite GI of ~68 (medium) driven by high-GI maida bhatura; dietary fibre 3.33 g/100 g from chickpeas partially moderates postprandial glucose response, but a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 90 g total carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to one bhatura and a moderate portion of chole, prioritise the chickpea component for its fibre and protein content, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the medium-GI composite and high total carbohydrate load per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying of bhatura contributes 10 g fat/100 g with 2.22 g saturated fat and 0.11 g trans fat; a full serving delivers approximately 45 g total fat and 0.5 g trans fat, which is clinically significant for cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit bhatura to one piece, request baked or pan-cooked bhatura where available to reduce fat load, and avoid reused frying oil which elevates trans fat content; the chickpea chole component is cardioprotective due to its fibre, plant protein, iron, and phytosterol content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may worsen joint inflammation; however, turmeric, ginger, and garlic in chole provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request baked or pan-cooked bhatura to reduce AGE exposure, monitor individual response to nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, green chillies), and avoid dishes prepared with reused frying oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chole Bhature (also spelled Choley Bathura or Chhole Bhature) is a classic North Indian dish originating from the Punjab region, widely popular across India and served in Indian restaurants throughout Singapore. The dish consists of two components: chole (a spiced chickpea curry) and bhatura (a large, fluffy, deep-fried leavened bread made from refined wheat flour/maida, yogurt, and a leavening agent). The bhatura is deep-fried until it puffs up into a golden, airy balloon with a slightly crisp exterior and soft, chewy interior. The chole is a robust, tangy, and mildly spicy curry made from boiled chickpeas simmered with tomatoes, onions, ginger, garlic, cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and amchur (dry mango powder), giving it a characteristic dark colour and complex flavour. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 189 kcal, 5.6 g protein, 10 g fat (2.2 g saturated), 20 g carbohydrate, 3.3 g dietary fibre, and 267 mg sodium. A standard serving of 450 g (2 bhatura + chole) delivers roughly 850 kcal and 1200 mg sodium. The dish is vegetarian and halal. Clinically, the high sodium content and deep-fried bhatura make it a moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease; the medium composite GI (~68) and high total carbohydrate per serving require portion control for Type 2 Diabetes management. The chickpea component contributes meaningful fibre, plant protein, iron, and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic), partially offsetting the less favourable profile of the fried bread component.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chole bhature?
Is chole bhature good for diabetics?
Can I eat chole bhature if I have high blood pressure?
How much sodium in chole bhature?
What is the GI of chole bhature?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Chole Bhature as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN component data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked chickpeas and fried bread analogues. The input record values are internally consistent with the expected macronutrient profile of this dish and are retained as the best available estimate. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN (chickpea curry and fried bread components) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked chickpeas FDC ID 173757; fried bread analogue). No single approved-source entry exists for Chole Bhature as a named composite dish. Source match type is 'estimated'. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single named entry; component-level data would require a full recipe-level calculation which is outside the scope of this record. 3. GI of ~68 is an analogous estimate based on weighted composite of chickpeas (GI ~28, University of Sydney GI Database) and deep-fried white wheat bread (GI ~75); direct GI testing of Chole Bhature is recommended. 4. Glycemic load per serving (450 g) estimated at 24.5 based on estimated GI of 68 and net available carbohydrate of approximately 36 g per half-plate portion. 5. The input record listed 'diabetes' as a health condition; this has been mapped to 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. 6. The input record listed 'rheumatoid_arthritis' with suitable=false but the reason text described anti-inflammatory benefits — this contradiction has been resolved: the condition is retained as moderate concern with nuanced guidance. 7. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 is an approximate match for the chickpea component only; FoodEx2 A00LC is used as a composite dish anchor and should be verified against the EFSA FoodEx2 browser. SNOMED CT gap acknowledged. 8. The input source (ICMR-NIN) is an approved primary source for Indian dishes and is retained; USDA FoodData Central is added as a cross-reference secondary source. 9. Q8 fix (iteration 3): self-check performed on aliases_by_language vs flat aliases array. All string values from aliases_by_language['english'] (9 entries), aliases_by_language['chinese'] (2 entries), aliases_by_language['malay'] (1 entry), aliases_by_language['tamil'] (1 entry), aliases_by_language['hindi'] (1 entry), and aliases_by_language['romanized'] (1 entry) verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Total flat aliases: 15 entries. No discrepancies found.
